schools.summary: Paul, gazing steadily at the council (with composed boldness, conscious of no guilt, Gill), claimed to have lived in all good conscience before God — asserting his sincerity, even in his former zeal as a persecutor when he acted wrongly but conscientiously (1 Timothy 1:13).
Henry: Paul's confidence before the Sanhedrin — protesting his integrity, conscious of no guilt, the boldness of a clear conscience before his judges.

themes: I have lived in all good conscience before God
the bold gaze and clear conscience

schools.summary: Ananias the high priest (a man of bad character, cruel and unjust, Robertson, Barnes) commanded Paul struck on the mouth — an illegal, provocative act (offensive at a Jew's hands, Robertson), to silence him, as Jesus had been struck (John 18:22).
Calvin: the high priest's rage unmasked — pouring out fury like a beast, the Lord stripping off his pretended gravity; the disorder of a council estranged from God.

themes: the high priest Ananias commanded them to smite him
the illegal blow on the mouth

schools.summary: Paul rebuked the high priest — 'God shall smite thee, thou whited wall' — for judging by the law yet violating it; 'God shall smite thee' a prediction not an imprecation (Barnes), fulfilled in Ananias's death; 'whited wall' the proverbial term for a hypocrite (Robertson); but Robertson sees an indignant outburst more self-control might have avoided.

Calvin defends it: not a curse but a sharp reprehension joined with denouncing punishment — a lawful accusation from a quiet mind, not railing, not passing Christ's bounds; Wesley: a prophetic impulse.

debates.question: Was Paul's 'whited wall' rebuke a sinful lapse or a justified prophetic denunciation?

sides.position: A lawful, just rebuke (or prophetic prediction) from a quiet mind, not railing
An indignant outburst; more self-control would have served Paul better (he confesses it, v.5)

themes: God shall smite thee, thou whited wall
sittest thou to judge me after the law

schools.summary: The bystanders rebuked Paul — 'revilest thou God's high priest?' — charging irreverence toward the office (which called for respect, Robertson, Deuteronomy 17); Barnes notes the irony that they appealed to the office's dignity though Ananias was not properly the high priest.
Calvin: the hypocrisy — scrutinizing Paul's sharp word yet winking at Ananias's illegal violence; looking narrowly into others' faults, excusing their own.

Paul replied 'I wist not that he was the high priest,' citing Exodus 22:28; most take it as literal ignorance — Paul long absent, the office vacant, Ananias not in robes or seat, genuinely not recognizing him (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, Ellicott), according with the historical vacancy.

Calvin (with Augustine) and Lightfoot favor irony — a taunting excuse: 'I acknowledge nothing in this man which belongeth to the priest'; Paul knowing the office but denying Ananias deserved its honor.

debates.question: What does Paul's 'I wist not that he was the high priest' mean?

sides.position: Literal ignorance — the office vacant, Ananias not recognizable, Paul long absent
Irony — Paul denying Ananias deserved the honor of the office
'I did not consider' — an apologetic recantation

themes: I wist not that he was the high priest
Thou shalt not speak evil of the ruler

schools.summary: Paul, perceiving the council part Pharisee and part Sadducee, cried that he was a Pharisee called in question for the resurrection — a stratagem to divide the council (Clarke), preventing a hasty unanimous condemnation as had doomed Stephen (Ellicott); the resurrection the true heart of the matter.
Chrysostom defends it: Paul wished to divide the multitude's evil unanimity, and spoke no falsehood — he was a Pharisee by descent and genuinely contended for the resurrection.

themes: I am a Pharisee, the son of a Pharisee
of the hope and resurrection am I called in question
the resurrection the heart of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul's words split the council — the Pharisees taking his side against the Sadducees (Barnes) — preventing the hasty unanimous condemnation (Ellicott); the dissension was strife, not mere disagreement (Robertson); the providence in the division breaking the council's evil unanimity.

themes: there arose a dissension between Pharisees and Sadducees

schools.summary: The Sadducees denied the resurrection, angels, and spirits — materialists holding the soul died with the body (Barnes) — while the Pharisees confessed both (resurrection and angels/spirits, grouped as 'both' because of their like nature, Robertson).
Calvin: the gravity of the Sadducees' error, a 'detestable monster' to be fled; affirming against all materialism the immortality of human and angelic spirits and the reality of the spiritual world.

themes: the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, neither angel, nor spirit

schools.summary: The Pharisees defended Paul — 'we find no evil' (a verdict of innocence like Pilate's on Jesus, Barnes) — suggesting a spirit or angel spoke to him (perverting his vision-statement, Barnes), their motive hatred of the Sadducees; 'let us not fight against God' echoing Gamaliel (5:39) but textually doubtful (Clarke, Ellicott), the Pharisees still halting between two opinions (Ellicott).

themes: the scribes of the Pharisees' part defended Paul
we find no evil in this man
if a spirit or angel hath spoken to him

schools.summary: As the strife grew violent (threatening to tear Paul apart, Robertson), the chief captain, fearing for the Roman citizen (Ellicott), commanded the soldiers to seize Paul and bring him to the castle — the second rescue from the murderous council.
Calvin: the providence — the captain, minister of God's providence, delivering Paul a second time, the Lord directing his blind hands to save his servant.

themes: the chief captain, fearing Paul should be pulled in pieces
take him by force into the castle
God's providence through the Roman power

schools.summary: The Lord Jesus stood by Paul (appearing in his crisis, Robertson) and said 'be of good cheer' — answering his anxious questions (Ellicott) — promising 'so must thou bear witness also at Rome,' the divine commission making the journey certain (Barnes, fulfilled 28:30-31).
Calvin: the comfort was not immediate deliverance but the assurance his trials would issue in witness at Rome — teaching us to rest on God's word even when help is delayed.

themes: the Lord stood by him, Be of good cheer
so must thou bear witness also at Rome

schools.summary: Certain Jews bound themselves under a curse to kill Paul, vowing not to eat or drink until they had — a desperate, murderous conspiracy (Henry); Lightfoot notes the rabbis could absolve such vows, so the conspirators risked little.
Chrysostom: the vehement, revengeful malice — fasting the mother of murder, the vow binding them to bloodshed under pretext of piety.

themes: neither eat nor drink till they had killed Paul

schools.summary: More than forty men joined the conspiracy — a large, determined band of assassins (Robertson), displaying the perverse unanimity in evil (Chrysostom); a formidable threat requiring the large escort that followed (v.23), underscoring the providence that delivered Paul.

themes: more than forty which had made this conspiracy

schools.summary: The conspirators came to the chief priests and elders, disclosing their vow to kill Paul and seeking their cooperation; Chrysostom marks the corruption — the leaders, who should have sought justice, joining a plot fit for banditti, not rulers.

themes: they came to the chief priests and elders
we have bound ourselves under a great curse
the leaders' collusion in the plot

schools.summary: The conspirators planned for the council to request Paul's appearance, under pretext of further inquiry, so they could kill him on the way — a treacherous scheme abusing legal forms to mask murder; the pretended inquiry cloaking the ambush.

themes: signify to the chief captain that he bring him down
as though ye would enquire something more perfectly
we are ready to kill him

schools.summary: Paul's sister's son (the only mention of his family) heard of the plot and came to the castle to warn Paul — a providential discovery (Chrysostom: again saved by man's forethought); the nephew's access to the castle suggesting Paul's confinement was not close.
Calvin: the divine care — though the Lord had promised Paul's safety (v.11), he used the nephew's warning as the means, God working through human forethought.

themes: Paul's sister's son heard of their lying in wait
entered into the castle, and told Paul
the only mention of Paul's family

schools.summary: Paul called a centurion and sent his nephew to the captain with the warning — acting prudently despite the Lord's promise (v.11); Chrysostom marks the balance: even after the vision, Paul was saved by human means, believing yet not sleeping, using the wisdom in his power.
Calvin: faith did not make Paul careless but diligent in using means — the harmony of trusting the promise and employing the means of preservation.

themes: Paul called a centurion, Bring this young man

schools.summary: The centurion took the young man to the captain, relaying Paul's request — Paul, though 'the prisoner,' treated with respect, his request honored (his Roman citizenship securing courteous treatment); the orderly relay of the warning, a link in the providential chain.

themes: Paul the prisoner called me, prayed me to bring this young man
respect shown Paul as a prisoner

schools.summary: The captain took the young man by the hand and led him aside privately, asking what he had to tell — a courteous, considerate manner, and a prudent privacy to learn the matter without alerting others.
Calvin: the providence in the captain's kindness — God governing his courteous care to preserve Paul.

themes: the chief captain took him by the hand, aside privately
What is that thou hast to tell me

schools.summary: The nephew reported the plot — the Jews' plan to request Paul's appearance under pretext of inquiry (the scheme of v.15) — faithfully conveying the conspiracy to the captain; the providence in the secret plot laid bare through a single witness.

themes: the Jews have agreed to desire thee to bring down Paul
as though they would enquire more perfectly

schools.summary: The nephew warned the captain not to yield — more than forty men in ambush, bound by oath, ready and awaiting only his consent — an earnest plea joined to the full disclosure; the warning coming just in time, the conspirators ready to strike.

themes: do not thou yield unto them
more than forty men lie in wait, bound by oath
the timely warning and urgent plea

schools.summary: The captain dismissed the young man, charging him to secrecy, that the conspirators not learn their plot was discovered (Ellicott, Robertson) — a prudent handling (Chrysostom), planning to act before they could adapt; the providence working through the captain's wise discretion.

themes: he let the young man depart, charged him to secrecy

schools.summary: The captain ordered a large escort — two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, two hundred spearmen, nearly five hundred men — to take Paul to Caesarea by night (the third hour, secretly and swiftly), taking the threat of forty assassins seriously; Chrysostom notes the irony, Paul escorted like a king.

themes: two hundred soldiers, seventy horsemen, two hundred spearmen
at the third hour of the night

schools.summary: The captain provided mounts for Paul and ordered him brought safely to Felix the governor at Caesarea — careful provision for the transfer to the proper authority, beyond the reach of the plot, toward the appeals that would carry Paul to Rome.

themes: provide them beasts to set Paul on
bring him safe unto Felix the governor

schools.summary: The captain wrote an official letter (an elogium accompanying the prisoner) to Felix, explaining Paul's case — a communication that would serve, providentially, as a testimony to Paul's innocence (Henry); introducing Lysias's letter (vv.26-30).

themes: he wrote a letter after this manner
providence in the letter testifying to innocence

schools.summary: The letter opens with the formal greeting — Claudius Lysias (the captain, who had purchased citizenship and taken the name Claudius, 22:28) to the most excellent Felix (the procurator of Judea) — following official epistolary form (as 15:23).

themes: Claudius Lysias unto the most excellent governor Felix
the named captain and titled governor

schools.summary: Lysias's account was self-serving and reversed the facts — claiming he rescued Paul 'having understood that he was a Roman,' though he learned of the citizenship only after, when about to scourge him (Ellicott, Robertson), omitting that illegal order to put himself in a good light with Felix.
Calvin: the providence even so — the captain, to gain favor for Paul, presents him as a wronged Roman, God governing the infidel's tongue for his servant's good.

debates.question: Is Lysias's account of rescuing Paul accurate?

sides.position: It reverses the facts — he learned of Paul's citizenship only after the rescue, and omits the illegal scourging order, to flatter himself; yet God overruled it for Paul's good

themes: I came with an army and rescued him
having understood that he was a Roman

schools.summary: Lysias reports that, seeking to learn the charge against Paul (Robertson: to know fully), he brought him before the Sanhedrin — the inquiry of 22:30-23:10, which ended in violent division; the captain's reasonable procedure to discover the accusation.

themes: I would have known the cause wherefore they accused him
I brought him forth into their council

schools.summary: Lysias perceived Paul was accused of Jewish religious questions, not a crime worthy of death or bonds — the distinction Gallio drew (18:14, Robertson) — declaring him innocent of any Roman crime; every Roman magistrate so judged Paul (Gallio, Lysias, Felix, Festus, Agrippa).
Calvin: the captain, a profane man, counted religious questions trivial, yet his verdict refuted the Jews' charges and vindicated Paul.

themes: accused of questions of their law
nothing worthy of death or of bonds

schools.summary: Lysias closes the letter, reporting the plot (the reason for the transfer) and directing the accusers to bring their charges before Felix; the formal 'farewell' closing the letter (as 15:29, Ellicott); the conspirators' fate unknown, likely considering themselves absolved when Paul was gone.
Calvin: the captain brings the adversaries into contempt — exposing their treacherous plot, which shows their cause unjust, for if it were lawful they would have trusted the law, not sought murder.

themes: the Jews laid wait for the man
I sent straightway to thee, and commanded his accusers

schools.summary: The soldiers took Paul by night to Antipatris (about forty miles, a hard night's ride, Robertson) — a swift march under cover of darkness, beyond the danger zone; Chrysostom: the large escort conveying Paul like a king, by night, for fear of the people's wrath.

themes: the soldiers brought him by night to Antipatris
the safe removal beyond the danger zone

schools.summary: At Antipatris, beyond the danger, the foot soldiers returned to Jerusalem, leaving the seventy horsemen to escort Paul to Caesarea (Barnes, Clarke) — the horsemen sufficing for the safer remaining journey.
Calvin: the captain's prudence — not sending the whole garrison far away, but only as far as needed for safety.

themes: they left the horsemen to go with him
beyond the danger of the conspirators
the orderly handling of the escort

schools.summary: The horsemen reached Caesarea, delivered Lysias's letter, and presented Paul to Felix — the orderly transfer to the proper authority, fulfilling Agabus's prophecy (21:11, Robertson: Paul delivered to the Gentiles); the safe arrival, the plot foiled.

themes: they came to Caesarea, delivered the epistle

schools.summary: Felix, having read the letter, asked Paul's province to determine jurisdiction (Barnes), learning he was of Cilicia (Paul's native province, Tarsus, 21:39) — a natural question (as Pilate's of Jesus, Luke 23:6, Ellicott), though Felix chose to take the case despite Paul belonging to another province.

themes: the governor read the letter, asked of what province
he understood that he was of Cilicia

schools.summary: Felix deferred the hearing until Paul's accusers arrived (ensuring both sides present, as Roman law required) and kept Paul in Herod's praetorium (the palace at Caesarea, now the governor's residence) — Paul again in custody (Chrysostom), awaiting trial; the orderly procedure and safe custody.

themes: I will hear thee when thine accusers are come
